section.title: Laws Concerning Vows
section.sectionRationale: Coming right after the festival calendar, this short law addresses a very personal question: what happens when someone makes a vow to the LORD. It matters enough to God that He gives careful, specific instruction protecting the seriousness of a spoken vow while also defining real, limited exceptions within a household.
section.thesis: A man's vow to the LORD must never be broken, and while a woman still under her father's or husband's authority may have her vow confirmed or nullified by him on the day he hears it, once that window passes, or once a vow stands unchallenged, it holds with the same binding weight as any other vow before God.
section.frameNote: Two movements: the unconditional binding force of a man's vow; and the specific, time-limited role a father or husband plays in confirming or nullifying the vow of a daughter or wife still under his household authority.

units.label: A vow must not be broken
units.note: he must do everything he has promised

verses.text: Then Moses said to the heads of the tribes of Israel, “This is what the LORD has commanded:
If a man makes a {{vow|vow}} to the LORD or swears an oath to obligate himself by a pledge, he must not break his word; he must do everything he has promised.

media.alt: A father and daughter standing quietly at a doorway on a still evening
media.caption: A vow made under a father's roof, confirmed by his silence.

verses.text: And if a woman in her father's house during her youth makes a vow to the LORD or obligates herself by a pledge,
and her father hears about her vow or pledge but says nothing to her, then all the vows or pledges by which she has bound herself shall stand.

But if her father prohibits her on the day he hears about it, then none of the vows or pledges by which she has bound herself shall stand. The LORD will absolve her because her father has prohibited her.

units.label: A wife's vow
units.note: her husband may confirm or {{nullify|nullify}} it

verses.text: If a woman marries while under a vow or rash promise by which she has bound herself,
and her husband hears of it but says nothing to her on that day, then the vows or pledges by which she has bound herself shall stand.
But if her husband prohibits her when he hears of it, he nullifies the vow that binds her or the rash promise she has made, and the LORD will absolve her.
Every vow a widow or divorced woman pledges to fulfill is binding on her.
If a woman in her husband's house has made a vow or put herself under an obligation with an oath,
and her husband hears of it but says nothing to her and does not prohibit her, then all the vows or pledges by which she has bound herself shall stand.

But if her husband nullifies them on the day he hears of them, then nothing that came from her lips, whether her vows or pledges, shall stand. Her husband has nullified them, and the LORD will absolve her.
Her husband may confirm or nullify any vow or any sworn pledge to deny herself.
But if her husband says nothing to her from day to day, then he confirms all the vows and pledges that bind her. He has confirmed them, because he said nothing to her on the day he heard about them.
But if he nullifies them after he hears of them, then he will bear her iniquity.”
These are the statutes that the LORD commanded Moses concerning the relationship between a man and his wife, and between a father and a young daughter still in his home.

vow.gloss: A vow, a voluntary promise made to God. A neder was never required, but once spoken, Scripture treats it with utmost seriousness, since it invokes God's own name and character as witness to the pledge, Deuteronomy 23:21-23.

nullify.gloss: To break, annul, or nullify. This law grants a father or husband a narrow, time-bound power to parar, nullify, a dependent woman's vow on the very day he learns of it, after which the window closes and the vow, if unchallenged, holds with full force.

basic.context: This short chapter gives Israel a specific law about vows, voluntary promises made to God. A man's vow always binds him completely. A woman still living under her father's or husband's authority has a narrow window in which he may nullify her vow, but if he says nothing on the day he hears it, or if any window passes, the vow stands exactly as binding as any other.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real legal statute regulating vows within Israel's specific household and covenant structure. It reflects the household authority patterns of its ancient context while still taking women's own spoken vows with real seriousness, since even a nullified vow required the LORD's own absolution, not merely a father's or husband's dismissal.
basic.originalLanguages: A neder, vow, once spoken to the LORD, carries binding weight because it calls on God's own name and character. The specific power given to a father or husband to parar, nullify, is narrow and time-limited, valid only on the very day he hears of it, after which his silence itself confirms the vow with full force.
basic.typology: The seriousness with which God treats every spoken word offered to Him anticipates Jesus' own teaching that a simple yes or no should be as binding as any oath, Matthew 5:33-37, since the character of the speaker, not elaborate vow-making, is what should guarantee truthful, faithful speech.
basic.christ: While this chapter contains no direct messianic prophecy, its insistence that a spoken vow to God must be honored completely reflects the same unwavering faithfulness to His own word that God shows throughout Scripture, ultimately embodied in Christ, who is Himself described as the amen, the faithful and true witness, in whom every promise of God is guaranteed, Revelation 3:14, 2 Corinthians 1:20.
basic.god: God takes human speech directed to Him with total seriousness, holding people to their word even when it costs them, while also building in real, limited protections for those under another's household authority. His concern for the vulnerable and His insistence on integrity of speech operate together in the same law.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 23:21-23 reinforces that a vow, though never required, must be kept once made. Ecclesiastes 5:4-5 warns that it is better not to vow than to vow and not fulfill it. Matthew 5:33-37 has Jesus teach that truthful, simple speech should make elaborate vows unnecessary altogether.
basic.oneStory: God cares deeply about the integrity of every word spoken to Him, holding people to their vows while making real, careful provision for those under another's authority. This concern for truthful speech runs through Scripture to Jesus, whose own faithful word never fails, and in whom every promise of God finds its unshakeable yes.

context.literary: This brief legal chapter sits between the festival calendar of chapters 28 to 29 and the war against Midian in chapter 31, addressing a very personal dimension of covenant faithfulness, the individual's own spoken word to God, right in the middle of Israel's national and military affairs.
context.historical: Within the patriarchal household structures of ancient Israel, women's vows could carry significant financial or personal obligations affecting the whole household's resources, which this law addresses by granting the household head a narrow, time-limited power of review rather than open-ended control.
context.canonical: This law becomes part of the broader biblical theology of vows and oaths, developed further in Deuteronomy, reflected on in Ecclesiastes and the Psalms, and ultimately addressed directly by Jesus in the Sermon on the Mount.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To establish the binding seriousness of vows made to God while providing a narrow, specific, time-limited protection for dependent women within the household structures of ancient Israel.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This chapter is case law specific to ancient Israel's household and legal structures; it does not prescribe identical household authority structures for believers today. Its abiding principle is the seriousness of keeping one's word before God, a principle Jesus later applies to all speech, not only formal vows.

debates.question: Does this law devalue women's spoken word before God?

views.label: The law protects, rather than devalues, women within their historical context
views.case: Far from ignoring a woman's vow, the text takes it seriously enough to require the LORD's own absolution even when nullified, and it protects young women and wives from being bound by financially or personally reckless promises within a household structure where they did not independently control resources.

views.label: The law reflects its ancient patriarchal context rather than a timeless pattern
views.case: The differential treatment by gender and household role reflects specific ancient Near Eastern social structures around household authority and resources, and its detailed provisions were never intended as a template for gender relations beyond that particular covenant and cultural setting.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that this passage treats a woman's vow as a real, serious act before God, requiring divine absolution even when set aside, and that the law's provisions are historically and culturally specific case law rather than a timeless prescription for household authority in every era.

study.originalLanguages: A neder, vow, spoken to the LORD, calls upon God's own character as witness and so binds the speaker completely once uttered. The specific power to parar, nullify, is deliberately narrow, restricted to the very day a father or husband first hears of the vow, after which continued silence itself functions as confirmation with full binding force.

typology.type: The absolute seriousness of a spoken vow to God
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates Jesus' teaching that truthful, simple speech should itself carry the weight of an oath, making elaborate vows unnecessary, Matthew 5:33-37.

typology.type: God's own faithfulness to every word He speaks
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ, called the Amen, the faithful and true witness, in whom every promise of God is guaranteed, Revelation 3:14, 2 Corinthians 1:20.

study.christ: This law's insistence that a vow spoken to the LORD must be kept reflects, in miniature, God's own unwavering faithfulness to His word, a faithfulness fully embodied in Christ, described in Revelation 3:14 as the Amen, the faithful and true witness. Where human vow-keeping remains imperfect and sometimes requires the very protections this law provides, Christ's own word never needs qualification, nullification, or protection; every promise of God finds its guaranteed yes in Him, 2 Corinthians 1:20.
study.god: God holds human speech directed toward Him to the highest standard of integrity, insisting a vow once made must be kept, while also caring enough for the vulnerable within a household to provide narrow, specific, time-bound protections. His concern for truthful speech and His concern for the vulnerable are not in tension but expressed together in the very same law.

crossReferences.note: Reinforces that though a vow is never required, once made it must be kept without delay.
Warns that it is better not to vow at all than to vow and fail to fulfill it.
Jesus teaches that truthful, simple speech should make elaborate vow-making unnecessary altogether.
Describes Christ as the Amen, the faithful and true witness, the ultimate guarantor of every divine promise.

study.oneStory: God takes every word spoken to Him with total seriousness, holding people to their vows while carefully protecting the vulnerable within a household structure. This concern for truthful, faithful speech runs through the whole Bible to Christ, the Amen, whose own word never fails and in whom every promise of God is guaranteed completely.

quiz.q: According to verse 2, what is required of a man who makes a vow to the LORD?

options.text: He must not break his word; he must do everything he has promised.
options.note: Verse 2 states this requirement directly and without qualification.

options.text: He may reconsider the vow at any time without consequence.
options.note: The text states the opposite, that his word must not be broken.

options.text: He must have the vow approved by the priest before it is binding.
options.note: No such priestly approval process is mentioned for a man's own vow.

options.text: No specific requirement is given.
options.note: Verse 2 gives a clear and direct requirement.

quiz.q: What happens to a young woman's vow if her father hears of it and says nothing, according to verse 4?

options.text: All her vows and pledges shall stand.
options.note: Verse 4 states this exactly.

options.text: The vow is automatically canceled.
options.note: The opposite is true; silence confirms the vow rather than canceling it.

options.text: The vow must be renewed publicly each year.
options.note: No such renewal requirement is mentioned in this law.

options.text: No outcome is specified.
options.note: Verse 4 clearly specifies the outcome.

quiz.q: Within what timeframe could a father or husband nullify a dependent woman's vow, according to this chapter?

options.text: Only on the day he first heard of it.
options.note: This time limit is repeated in verses 5, 7, 8, and 12.

options.text: At any point during her lifetime.
options.note: The law restricts this power specifically to the day he first hears of the vow.

options.text: Only within one year of the vow being made.
options.note: The text specifies the single day he hears of it, not a full year.

options.text: No time limit is given.
options.note: The single-day limit is stated repeatedly throughout the chapter.

quiz.q: According to verse 9, whose vow was always fully binding regardless of any household authority?

options.text: The vow of a widow or divorced woman.
options.note: Verse 9 states this explicitly.

options.text: The vow of a young daughter still in her father's house.
options.note: Her vow could be nullified by her father within the specified timeframe, unlike the vow described in verse 9.

options.text: The vow of a married woman still under her husband's authority.
options.note: Her vow could similarly be nullified by her husband within the specified timeframe.

options.text: No exception to household authority is mentioned.
options.note: Verse 9 states this exception directly.

quiz.q: According to verse 15, what happens if a husband nullifies his wife's vow well after the day he first heard of it?

options.text: He himself bears the guilt of her broken vow.
options.note: Verse 15 states this consequence directly.

options.text: The vow is nullified with no further consequence to anyone.
options.note: The text specifies that delayed nullification transfers the guilt to the husband himself.

options.text: The wife alone bears full responsibility regardless of timing.
options.note: The text specifically shifts responsibility to the husband in this case of delayed nullification.

options.text: No consequence is mentioned for late nullification.
options.note: Verse 15 addresses this consequence explicitly.

questions: This law treats every vow spoken to God with total seriousness. Where in your own life do you speak commitments to God more casually than you should?
A father or husband's silence itself functioned as confirmation of a vow. Where might your own silence be quietly confirming a commitment you have not fully thought through?
Jesus later taught that simple, truthful speech should make elaborate vows unnecessary. How consistent is your everyday yes and no with what you actually follow through on?
This law provided real protection for those under another's household authority. Where do you need to extend care and protection to someone more vulnerable than yourself?
Christ is called the Amen, the one whose word never fails. Where do you need to rest more fully in the certainty that His promises to you will be kept completely?

translationNotes.note: Vow and oath, though closely related, are technically distinct in Hebrew law, a neder typically dedicating something to God while a shevuah, oath, is a sworn pledge; this chapter treats both with the same binding seriousness.

The LORD will absolve her, in cases of a nullified vow, shows that even a rightly canceled vow required God's own release, underscoring that a vow's binding power flowed from its being made to Him, not merely from household regulation.

The closing summary explicitly limits the law's scope to relationships between a husband and wife and between a father and an unmarried daughter still in his household, showing the text itself frames this as household-specific case law rather than a universal statute governing all vows.
